Re: 
Paul Balalis -
FPPC Advice Request 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
This office is requesting advice from the FPPC concerning Paul 
Balalis, a member of the Planning Commission for the City of 
Newport Beach. 
Since January 19, 1982, Mr. Balalis has served as Chairman of the 
Board of Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank, and is the 
owner of approximately 6% of the outstanding shares of stock 
issued by that institution. 
Prior to taking this position, and 
from February 7, 1980, Mr. Balalis was a member of the Board of 
Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank. 
This letter is prompted by Mr. Balalis' recent disclosure that he 
voted on, and participated in the discussions concerning, a Use 
Permit Application submitted by a business with which the bank 
had placed approximately $240,000.00 in loans. 
A more thorough 
discussion of the relevant facts follows: 
Magic Island is the name given to a private club located in the 
City of Newport Beach that attracts members by offering top-
quality magic acts, food and a certain ambiance. 
The business 
commenced operation on or about September 4, 1981, appoximately 
one year after the Planning Commission, with Mr. Balalis' vote 
and participation, approved their Use Permit Application. 
A Use 
Permit is a prerequisite to the operation of any restaurant or 
recreational establishment within the City of Newport Beach. 
I'-. 1)" 
: ' { \ 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
March 9, 1982 
Page Two 
Re: 
Paul Balalis -
FPPC Advice Request 
On February 4, 1982, the Planning Commission considered an 
Application by Magic Island to amend the provisions of the pre-
viously granted Use Permit, to allow for daytime use of the 
facility as well as expansion of the nighttime operation. The 
Planning Commission approved, on a temporary basis, the daytime 
use of the facility, and continued the other discretionaryt 
approvals requested by the business. 
On or about May 12, 1981, Newport Harbor National Bank placed a 
loan in the sum of $200,000.00 with Magic Island, the loan 
guaranteed by Michael Callie and Sally Wrainswaite. 
It is the 
understanding of this office that Mr. Callie and Ms. Wrainswaite 
are principals in the operation and that both have an ownership 
interest. 
This loan was renewed November 13, 1981. 
On or about Auust 26, 1981, a loan in the sum of $100,000.00 was 
placed with Michael Callie and secured by real property owned by 
Mr. Callie. 
This loan was paid September 26, 1981. 
Each of these loans was made at or above the prime interest rate 
in effect at the time and, subject to a determination of credit 
worthiness, were available to other businesses or individuals on 
the same or similar terms. 
Finally, on April 29, 1981, a loan in the sum of $100,000.00 was 
made to Michael Callie, which loan was renewed October 30, 1981 
and again on January 19, 1982. 
On or about October 30, 1981, a 
loan in the sum of $42,000.00 was placed by Newport Harbor 
National Bank, with Magic Island and renewed January 18, 1982. 
As we have indicated above, Mr. Balalis is Chairman of the Board 
of Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank. 
The Board, as is 
customary, makes policy, with respect to operations, in the broad 
sense, and with respect to the manner in which the lending insti-
tution proposes to expand its operations. 
The Board of Directors 
does not approve loans, but after loans are made, the Board of 
Directors is given information concerning the size of the loan, 
the person or entity with whom the loan was placed and other 
relevant information. 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
March 9, 1982 
Page Three 
Re: 
Paul Balalis -
FPPC Advice Request 
The process by which loans are placed by the bank is as 
, 
follows: 
Loan applications are presented to a Loan Officer. 
The 
Loan Officer then reviews the application, acquires all relevant 
information, then makes a preliminary determination whether to 
approve or deny the loan request. 
The preliminary determination 
by the Loan Officer is then reviewed by a senior Loan Office, who 
has authority to approve loans, up to $420,000.00. 
Loans to be 
placed that are in excess of $420,000.00 are reviewed, and grant-
ed, by a Loan Committee. 
Newport Harbor National Bank has approximately 400 loans 
outstanding at this time, the total value of which is 
approximately $30,000,000.00. 
The total value of the assets of 
the institution is approximately $35,000,000.00. 
Mr. Balalalis served on the Loan Committee from February 7, 1980 
to March 27, 1981, but has had no involvement with the Loan 
Committee since that date. 
Mr. Balalis has advised this office 
that he first became aware of the loan commitments to Magic 
Island and principles thereof, on or about February 20, 1982. 
As Chairman of the Board, Mr. Balalis receives $250.00 per 
meeting, and the Board, customarily, meets once a month. 
It is 
unknown at this time, if, and when, dividends are to be paid on 
outstanding shares of stock owned by Mr. Balalis and other share-
holders. 
One final note, individual Board members who encourage 
the placement of loans to persons or entities who are not credit 
worthy may be held personally liable for any losses incurred by 
the lending institution as a result of the loan. 
Your advice is requested on the following questions: 
I. 
We have advised the Planning Commission and Mr. Balalis 
that his vote and participation in matters involving 
Magic Island, that occurred prior to the time he became 
aware of the relationship between Magic Island and 
Newport Harbor National Bank, does not invalidate the 
proceedings because, at the time of the participation 
and vote by Mr. Balalis, he did not know, or have reason 
to know, that he had a financial interest in the matter, 
and there has been no challenge to the decision of the 
Planning Commission; 


, Ms. Barbara Milma 
March 9, 1982 
Page Four 
Re: 
Paul Balalis -
FPPC Advice Request 
II. 
Is Mr. Balalais precluded from further participation in 
matters involving Magic Island by virtue of the provis-
ions of Section 87100 and 87103 A, C and/or D; 
III. 
Given the size of this lending institution, are there 
any guidelines that would assist Mr. Ba1ala1is in deter-
mining what size of loan commitment, or other business 
dealing, that will require disqualification; 
IV. 
Will Mr. Balalalis be precluded from participating in 
matters involving persons who have money on deposit with 
the bank, given the difficult in learning the identity 
of these persons from day-to-day and the fact that 
interest rates on deposits are essentially fixed by the 
Federal Government~ 
V. 
To what extent is Mr. Balala1is obligated to seek 
information as to the identify of borrowers, depositors 
and others who have business dealings with this lending 
institution? 
Please contact this office if you are of the opinion that further 
information is required to answer somme or all of the questions 
set forth above. 
Verr truly yours ~

Coastal Dependent Uses 
The City of Newport Beach is a unique coastal community with resources 
which include the Harbor, beaches, and many types of recreational 
facilities. 
The City is al~o characterized by waterfront homes and 
restaurants s industrial and commercial marine uses s and opportuni-
ties for coastal views. 
The desireability of the City as a water-
oriented community is also a factor in the desire of non-marine 
uses to locate within the Citys even where such use is not water 
related. 
While the Coastal Act does not require a separate component dealing 
with coastal-dependent development, such uses are integral to the 
I 
character and ambience of the City of Newport Beach. 
They contri-
bute greatly to the desireability of the City as a regional recrea-
II 
tional s industrial, commercial and financial center. 
Development 
I 
I 
pressures from competing land uses for limited oceanfront and bay-
front resources are being experienced, and it can be assumed that 
such pressures will continue to occur. 
Considerable diversity exists along the Newport Beach shoreline 
I 
with regard to existing land use and character. 
There is the poten-

tial for the location of new or replacement coastal-dependent and 
coastal-related land uses, as well as preservation and enhancement 
of existing marine-related uses. 
Policies in regards to coastal-dependent development follow: 
1. 
Coastal dependent developments shall have priority 
over other developments on or near the shoreline. 
Coastally-dependent uses shall be defined as those 
uses which require a site on or adjacent to the sea 
to be able to function at all.

2. 
Commercially and industrially zoned areas in the Coastal 
Zone shall be designated for coastally-dependent, coast-
ally-related and visitor serving uses as priority uses. 
IICoastally-related development
ll means any use that is 
dependent on a coastal-dependent development or use. 
3. 
Facilities which serve commercial fishing shall be 
encouraged and maintained unless the demand for the 
facilities no longer exist. 
4. 
In view of the City's attractions as a regional and 
statewide recreation area, the growing regional and 
statewide demand for water-oriented recreational facil-
ities, and the limited capacity of the City's harbor 
and oceanfront resources to fully satisfy such demands, 
the City shall encourage the opening and development of 
adjoining ocean and waterfront areas outside Newport 
Bay, so long as any adverse environmental impacts are 
mitigated, in a manner which may best serve to distri-
bute the increasing public need for water-oriented 
recreational facilities. 
5. 
The City shall protect and maintain the necessary 
support facilities and services for marine recreational 
and educational activities inlcuding: 
Marine ways and 
services, launching facilities, gas and pump-out stations, 
parking facilities, restrooms, showers, concessions 
and educational facilities such as the Sea Scout Base 
and the Intercollegiate Rowing Facilities. 
6. 
The City shall protect and enhance public visual access 
to the waterfront. 
New development (including land-
scaping), public or private, shall be sited and designed 
to protect public views of ocean and other coastal 
scenic areas.

Public Property Leaseholds 
Within the City of Newport Beach. there are many publicly-held 
lands. 
Some of these parcels are held in fee by the City. 
Some 
are held by the City as a tidelands trust. 
Over the years most of 
these areas have been leased and uses established on the properties. 
While it is not reasonable to expect long-established uses to be 
eliminated, the following lease hold policies are established to 
guide the leasing and re-leasing of public properties~ 
1. 
Any new leasing of public lands for private use shall 
be consistent with policies of the California Coastal 
Act and shall be subject to voter approval. 
2. 
In the leasing or re-leasing of publicly owned land, 
full consideration shall be given to the public's 
right of access to the ocean, beach. and bay and 
to the provision of coastal-dependent uses adjacent 
to the water 
3. 
At the time the leases are negotiated or renewed, care-
ful consideration shall be given to the consistency of 
the proposed use with the public interest. 
If a posi-
tive determination is made, the property shall be 
leased at fair market value, unless it is clearly in 
the public interest to do otherwise. 
4. 
All new leases and lease renewals of publicly owned 
land shall be considered by the City Council at a 
public hearing.

Policies relating to Visitor Serving Facilities follow: 
1. 
The City shall preserve, in its natural state, the ocean 
beaches, water, surf action, and coastal shoreline in 
a manner that will ensure their availability for continued 
public use and enjoyment. 
Proposals for the construction 
of anti-erosion structures, off-shore breakwaters, or 
future marinas shall be examined in light of this policy. 
2. 
The City shall also preserve and enhance the Upper Bay 
and marine coastal preserves which support varied species 
of plant, marine and wildlife populations to ensure their 
availability and continued use for ecological, educational 
and aesthetic purposes. 
3. 
Consistent with all other policies to protect and enhance 
the quality residential character of the community, the 
City shall encourage and protect both public and private 
water-oriented recreational and entertainment facilities 
as a means of providing public access to the waterfront. 
4. 
Provision and maintainence of public restroom facilities 
is top priority; the City Council should immediately 
commence planning and acquisitions necessary in order that 
public restrooms with appropriate signing be constructed 
as soon as possible; the size and location of the facilities 
shall take the residents into consideration ..

Jetty Pa rk.

4E.

public to restrooms, access points, and boat launching. 
5. 
The Oceanfront sidewalk shall be extended from "EII Street 
to the Wedge. 
SA. 
The Oceanfront sidewalk shall be extended from 1136thll 
Street to the Santa Ana River. 
6. 
Adequate marine sanitation facilities, including pump out 
stations and conveniently located public restrooms in the 
Harbor Area, shall be provided in order to reduce the risks 
of water pollution and health problems.

Drinking fountains shall be provided in conjunction with 
restroom facilities as a means of insuring their distri-
bution throughout the Coastal Zone, while minimizing the 
costs of extending water lines. 
Also, exterior showers 
shall be provided in conjunction with these facilities.

Bicycle racks shall be provided throughout the Coastal

If available, locking, pay-your-way bicycle racks 
shall be used. 
9. 
The City shall encourage and support the provision of 
guest slips, mooring and anchorages in Newport Harbor, 
and shall continue to work with the Orange County Harbor 
Department to provide these facilities where feasible. 
10. 
The City of Newport Beach currently provides overnight 
accommodations in all price ranges. 
The City shall 
encourage the maintenance of overnight accommodations 
in all price ranges, including encouraging the mainten-
ance of existing summer weekly rentals as a means of 
providing overnight accommodations. 
11. 
The City of Newport Beach shall encourage the County of 
Orange to provide additional recreational vehica1 camp 
areas in the Newport Dunes areas as a means of providing 
additional overnight accommodations. 
12. 
Development of the lower part of Bayview Landing should 
be coordinated with development in Newport Dunes to 
offer an expanded recreational vehicle park. 
13. 
The City of Newport Beach shall protect and encourage 
the maintenance of lower-cost commercial visitor serving 
facilities. 
14. 
The City shall actively pursue the provision of a public 
launching facility for small sailboats on lower Newport 
Bay along with the required parking.

AREA DESCRIPTIONS 
Balboa Peninsula Point 
Balboa Peninsula Point consists of the residential neighborhood 
between 
II A" Street and the harbor entrance on the Balboa Peninsula. 
The area is designated primarily for "Low-Density Residential" with 
some areas designated for "Multiple-Family Residential" uses. 
The public oceanfront beach and the beach at "N" Street on the 
bay have been designated "Recreational and Environmental Open 
Space." 
New proposals for this area include extension of the 
oceanfront public bikeway/walkway from "E" Street to The Wedge 
and addition of a restroom facility at the West Jetty Park (The 
Wedge). 
The bikeway/walkway shall be designed and located so as 
to minimize the impacts on adjacent residential uses (e.g., provide 
a buffer between residential uses and sidewalk). 
Central Balboa 
Central Balboa consists of the commercially-developed area between 
"A" Street and Coronado Street. 
This area is a unique mixture of 
visitor-oriented and neighborhood-oriented retail and service uses; 
including coastal-dependent visitor uses such as sport fishing 
establishments, day-boat rentals, ferry service, fishing docks, 
and historic Balboa Pavilion. 
Those areas which are near the bay 
and oceanfronts are shown for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" 
uses. 
Those areas which line East Balboa Boulevard have been 
designated "Retail and Service Commercial." 
It is the intent of these land use designations to encourage the 
continuation of the neighborhood and visitor-serving orientation

acre to be transferred to Newport Center. 
This leaves this site 
to function as open space, as shown on the LCP Land Use Plan. 
Mouth of Big Canyon 
The mouth of Big Canyon is shown on the LCP Land Use Plan as "Rec-
reational and Environmental Open Space." 
This area is to be pre-
served in its natural state to enhance the Upper Newport Bay 
Ecological Reserve. 
Newporter North 
This site, located on Jamboree Road northerly of the Newporter Inn, 
is designated for "Low-Density Residential" uses at 4 DU's per 
buildable acre, with 25% of the allowable units transferable to 
either Newport Center or North Ford at the option of the property 
owner. 
The structures shall be clustered to accommodate archaeo-
logical sites and marsh sites. 
A public bikeway/walkway is proposed 


- 52-
for this site. 
Newporter Inn 
The Newporter Inn site has been designated for "Recreational and 
r~arine Commercial ll uses in order to promote the continuation of the 
hotel use and allow expansion if needed. 
Newport Center 
Approximately one-third of the Newport Center site falls within 
the Coastal Zone. 
Most of the area is occupied by the Irvine 
Coast Country Club, shown as IIRecreational and Environmental Open 
Space ll on the Land Use Plan. 
The Marriott Hotel site is designated 
for IIAdministrative, Professional and Financial Commercial
ll uses 
to reflect the hotel use on the site. 
Permitted office uses on 
Corporate Plaza West and the Chamber of Commerce sites are shown 
by the IIAdministrative, Professional and Financial Commercial
ll 
designation, and residential uses are shown on the Sea Island and 
Coast Highway/Jamboree sites by the IIMedium Density Residential!! 
designation. 
Bayview Landing. 
This site adjacent to the Newport Dunes area is designated IIRecrea-
tional and Environmental Open Space 'l for public recreation uses with 
a view park and bike path on the upper portion, and R-V camping on 
the lower portion or similar recreational uses of no greater 
intensity. 
As an alternate use, 4 DU's per buildable acre will be 
permitted to be located on the lower portion of the site to preserve 
existing public views. 
I' 


I 
-53-
De Anza Trailer Park 
The De Anza Trailer Park is shown for "Multi-Family Residential" 
uses. 
It is the intent of the City to preserve the trailer park 
use as a means of providing a variety of housing opportunities 
within the City. 
The existing number of mobile home spaces shall 
be maintained by the property owner wither on-site or at another 
site in the City of Newport Beach. 
Beacon Bay 
The Beacon Bay area is a City-owned parcel which is currently 
leased and used for both residential and marine commercial uses. 
The LCP Land Use Plan designations reflect existing uses. 
It is 
the intent of the land use designations to preserve the existing 
land use pattern, particularly the marine commercial uses in the 
Balboa Yacht Basin Area. 
Coast Highwal/Bayside Drive 
This site is currently developed with three major restaurants (the 
Reuben E. Lee, Reuben's, and Isadore's), as well as a yacht broker 
and parking for the docking facilities. 
This area has been desig-
nated for "Recreational and Marine Commercial." 
In addition the 
area to be vacated upon completion of the Newport Bay bridge, shall 
be utilized for a boat launching facility. 
Bayside and Marine 
This vacant site at the entrance to Balboa Island is shown for 
"Recreational and Ma.rine Commercial." 
It is the intent of this 
designation to provide an appropriate land use to be consistent 
with the existing project approved for the site. 
If the office 
project approved for the site cannot be developed, every effort 
shall be made to acquire the site for public open space. 


- 54-
Balboa Island 
No changes to the existing land use pattern on Balboa Island are 
proposed. 
The Marine Avenue and Agate Avenue commercial districts 
are shown for "Retail and Service Commercial" to encourage contin-
uation of the existing patterns. 
Office and residential uses shall 
be prohibited on the ground level, but may be allowed on the second 
level where the ground level is occupied by a primary use which 
provides goods or services directly to the public. 
The submerged 
areas utilized by the Ferry Landing and fuel docks have been 
designated "Recreational and Marine Commercial." 
Only coastally-
dependent uses will be permitted on these bay sites. 
Bayside Drive 
The commercial areas along Bayside Drive below Irvine Terrace are 
shown for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" uses allowing con-
tinuation of the yacht clubs and the Orange County Harbor Depart-
ment facility. 
If redevelopment should occur, uses shall be limited 
to those allowed by this designation. 
Corona del Mar/Coast Highway Commercial 
The Corona del Mar commercial area along Coast Highway is shown for 
"Retail and Service Commercial" uses. 
This area functions primarily 
as a neighborhood commercial area and no changes are being proposed. 
Upper level offices may be permitted consistent with the Retail and 
Service Commercial designation. 
¬£orona del Mar State Beach 
The Corona del Mar State Beach is a primary visitor destination 


\'/ i t h i nth e Cit y 0 f New p 0 r t B e a c h ‚ÄĘ 
The des i g nat ion for 
II R e c rea t ion a 1 
and Environmental Open Space" is consistent with the existing use. 
Inspiration Point 
The existing publicly-held parcels, as well as the two undeveloped 
private parcels at Inspiration Point, have been shown for "Recrea-
tional and Environmental Open Space." 
These bluffs constitute a 
resource ~ State-wide significance and as such it is proposed that 
any privately-held vacant parcels in this area be acquired with 
State Coastal Conservancy Funds in order that they be preserved 
for the enjoyment of the public. 
Newport Beach Marine Life Refuge 
The Newport Beach Marine Li fe Refuge is shown for "Recreational and 
Environmental Open Space." 
The purpose of the Marine Life Refuge 
r 
is to conserve the natural flora and fauna resources of the shore-
I 
line, and to protect the marine life in the tide pool areas. 
I 
Buck Gully/Morning Canyon 
The Buck Gully and Morni ng Canyon areas have been shown for"Recrea-
tional and Envi ronmental Open Space" to be preserved in a natural 
state. 
It is proposed that the setbacks of residential lots abut-
ting these areas be adjusted to prevent alteration of the natural 
canyons. 
Upper Newport Bay Ecological Reserve 
This area is designated for "Recreational and Environmental Open 
Space," with detailed plans to be developed by the State Depart-
ment of Fish and Game.

LAND USE MAPS 
The Land Use Maps which follow illustrate the proposed LPC Land 
Use designations for the Coastal Zone portion of the City. 
The 
City's existing zoning maps are used as a base, with the proposed 
LCP designation as an overlay. 
The existing zoning is illustrated 
with letters (e.g., R-l, R-2, C-l, etc.) and the proposed LCP 
designation is shown with overlay patterns indexed at the bottom 
of each map. 
Existing land use is not shown. 
In many cases the proposed LCP Land Use will not be consistent with 
respect to the LCP zoning regulations when adopted, and would 
probably require Use Permit review for any proposed expansion where 
inconsistencies exist. 
The City's existing zoning ordinance has 
similar provisions for review of nonconforming uses when expanded.

Magic Island is the name given to a private club located in the

quality magic acts, food and a certain ambiance.

On February 4, 1982, the Planning Commission considered an 
Application by Magic Island to amend the provisions of the pre-
viously granted Use Permit, to allow for daytime use of the 
facility as well as expansion of the nighttime operation. The 
Planning Commission approved, on a temporary basis, the daytime 
use of the facility, and continued the other discretionaryt 
approvals requested by the business. 
On or about May 12, 1981, Newport Harbor National Bank placed a 
loan in the sum of $200,000.00 with Magic Island, the loan 
guaranteed by Michael Callie and Sally Wrainswaite. 
It is the 
understanding of this office that Mr. Callie and Ms. Wrainswaite 
are principals in the operation and that both have an ownership 
interest. 
This loan was renewed November 13, 1981. 
On or about Auust 26, 1981, a loan in the sum of $100,000.00 was 
placed with Michael Callie and secured by real property owned by 
Mr. Callie. 
This loan was paid September 26, 1981. 
Each of these loans was made at or above the prime interest rate 
in effect at the time and, subject to a determination of credit 
worthiness, were available to other businesses or individuals on 
the same or similar terms. 
Finally, on April 29, 1981, a loan in the sum of $100,000.00 was 
made to Michael Callie, which loan was renewed October 30, 1981 
and again on January 19, 1982. 
On or about October 30, 1981, a 
loan in the sum of $42,000.00 was placed by Newport Harbor 
National Bank, with Magic Island and renewed January 18, 1982. 
As we have indicated above, Mr. Balalis is Chairman of the Board 
of Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank. 
The Board, as is 
customary, makes policy, with respect to operations, in the broad 
sense, and with respect to the manner in which the lending insti-
tution proposes to expand its operations. 
The Board of Directors

The process by which loans are placed by the bank is as 
, 
follows: 
Loan applications are presented to a Loan Officer. 
The 
Loan Officer then reviews the application, acquires all relevant 
information, then makes a preliminary determination whether to 
approve or deny the loan request. 
The preliminary determination 
by the Loan Officer is then reviewed by a senior Loan Office, who 
has authority to approve loans, up to $420,000.00. 
Loans to be 
placed that are in excess of $420,000.00 are reviewed, and grant-
ed, by a Loan Committee. 
Newport Harbor National Bank has approximately 400 loans 
outstanding at this time, the total value of which is 
approximately $30,000,000.00. 
The total value of the assets of 
the institution is approximately $35,000,000.00. 
Mr. Balalalis served on the Loan Committee from February 7, 1980 
to March 27, 1981, but has had no involvement with the Loan 
Committee since that date. 
Mr. Balalis has advised this office 
that he first became aware of the loan commitments to Magic 
Island and principles thereof, on or about February 20, 1982. 
As Chairman of the Board, Mr. Balalis receives $250.00 per 
meeting, and the Board, customarily, meets once a month. 
It is 
unknown at this time, if, and when, dividends are to be paid on 
outstanding shares of stock owned by Mr. Balalis and other share-
holders. 
One final note, individual Board members who encourage 
the placement of loans to persons or entities who are not credit 
worthy may be held personally liable for any losses incurred by 
the lending institution as a result of the loan.

Coastal Dependent Uses 
The City of Newport Beach is a unique coastal community with resources 
which include the Harbor, beaches, and many types of recreational 
facilities. 
The City is al~o characterized by waterfront homes and 
restaurants s industrial and commercial marine uses s and opportuni-
ties for coastal views. 
The desireability of the City as a water-
oriented community is also a factor in the desire of non-marine 
uses to locate within the Citys even where such use is not water 
related. 
While the Coastal Act does not require a separate component dealing 
with coastal-dependent development, such uses are integral to the 
I 
character and ambience of the City of Newport Beach. 
They contri-
bute greatly to the desireability of the City as a regional recrea-
II 
tional s industrial, commercial and financial center. 
Development 
I 
I 
pressures from competing land uses for limited oceanfront and bay-
front resources are being experienced, and it can be assumed that 
such pressures will continue to occur. 
Considerable diversity exists along the Newport Beach shoreline 
I 
with regard to existing land use and character. 
There is the poten-

tial for the location of new or replacement coastal-dependent and 
coastal-related land uses, as well as preservation and enhancement 
of existing marine-related uses. 
Policies in regards to coastal-dependent development follow: 
1. 
Coastal dependent developments shall have priority 
over other developments on or near the shoreline. 
Coastally-dependent uses shall be defined as those 
uses which require a site on or adjacent to the sea 
to be able to function at all.

2. 
Commercially and industrially zoned areas in the Coastal 
Zone shall be designated for coastally-dependent, coast-
ally-related and visitor serving uses as priority uses. 
IICoastally-related development
ll means any use that is 
dependent on a coastal-dependent development or use. 
3. 
Facilities which serve commercial fishing shall be 
encouraged and maintained unless the demand for the 
facilities no longer exist. 
4. 
In view of the City's attractions as a regional and 
statewide recreation area, the growing regional and 
statewide demand for water-oriented recreational facil-
ities, and the limited capacity of the City's harbor 
and oceanfront resources to fully satisfy such demands, 
the City shall encourage the opening and development of 
adjoining ocean and waterfront areas outside Newport 
Bay, so long as any adverse environmental impacts are 
mitigated, in a manner which may best serve to distri-
bute the increasing public need for water-oriented 
recreational facilities. 
5. 
The City shall protect and maintain the necessary 
support facilities and services for marine recreational 
and educational activities inlcuding: 
Marine ways and 
services, launching facilities, gas and pump-out stations, 
parking facilities, restrooms, showers, concessions 
and educational facilities such as the Sea Scout Base 
and the Intercollegiate Rowing Facilities. 
6. 
The City shall protect and enhance public visual access 
to the waterfront. 
New development (including land-
scaping), public or private, shall be sited and designed 
to protect public views of ocean and other coastal 
scenic areas.

Public Property Leaseholds 
Within the City of Newport Beach. there are many publicly-held 
lands. 
Some of these parcels are held in fee by the City. 
Some 
are held by the City as a tidelands trust. 
Over the years most of 
these areas have been leased and uses established on the properties. 
While it is not reasonable to expect long-established uses to be 
eliminated, the following lease hold policies are established to 
guide the leasing and re-leasing of public properties~ 
1. 
Any new leasing of public lands for private use shall 
be consistent with policies of the California Coastal 
Act and shall be subject to voter approval. 
2. 
In the leasing or re-leasing of publicly owned land, 
full consideration shall be given to the public's 
right of access to the ocean, beach. and bay and 
to the provision of coastal-dependent uses adjacent 
to the water 
3. 
At the time the leases are negotiated or renewed, care-
ful consideration shall be given to the consistency of 
the proposed use with the public interest. 
If a posi-
tive determination is made, the property shall be 
leased at fair market value, unless it is clearly in 
the public interest to do otherwise. 
4. 
All new leases and lease renewals of publicly owned 
land shall be considered by the City Council at a 
public hearing.

Policies relating to Visitor Serving Facilities follow: 
1. 
The City shall preserve, in its natural state, the ocean 
beaches, water, surf action, and coastal shoreline in 
a manner that will ensure their availability for continued 
public use and enjoyment. 
Proposals for the construction 
of anti-erosion structures, off-shore breakwaters, or 
future marinas shall be examined in light of this policy. 
2. 
The City shall also preserve and enhance the Upper Bay 
and marine coastal preserves which support varied species 
of plant, marine and wildlife populations to ensure their 
availability and continued use for ecological, educational 
and aesthetic purposes. 
3. 
Consistent with all other policies to protect and enhance 
the quality residential character of the community, the

water-oriented recreational and entertainment facilities 
as a means of providing public access to the waterfront.

is top priority; the City Council should immediately 
commence planning and acquisitions necessary in order that 
public restrooms with appropriate signing be constructed 
as soon as possible; the size and location of the facilities 
shall take the residents into consideration ..

The City of Newport Beach shall protect and encourage

14. 
The City shall actively pursue the provision of a public 
launching facility for small sailboats on lower Newport 
Bay along with the required parking.

Balboa Peninsula Point 
Balboa Peninsula Point consists of the residential neighborhood 
between 
II A" Street and the harbor entrance on the Balboa Peninsula. 
The area is designated primarily for "Low-Density Residential" with 
some areas designated for "Multiple-Family Residential" uses. 
The public oceanfront beach and the beach at "N" Street on the 
bay have been designated "Recreational and Environmental Open 
Space." 
New proposals for this area include extension of the 
oceanfront public bikeway/walkway from "E" Street to The Wedge 
and addition of a restroom facility at the West Jetty Park (The 
Wedge). 
The bikeway/walkway shall be designed and located so as 
to minimize the impacts on adjacent residential uses (e.g., provide 
a buffer between residential uses and sidewalk). 
Central Balboa 
Central Balboa consists of the commercially-developed area between

This area is a unique mixture of 
visitor-oriented and neighborhood-oriented retail and service uses;

establishments, day-boat rentals, ferry service, fishing docks, 
and historic Balboa Pavilion. 
Those areas which are near the bay 
and oceanfronts are shown for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" 
uses. 
Those areas which line East Balboa Boulevard have been 
designated "Retail and Service Commercial." 
It is the intent of these land use designations to encourage the 
continuation of the neighborhood and visitor-serving orientation

erly twelve acres have a secondary alternate land use of IlRetail 
and Service Commercial,1I not to exceed 52,727 square feet. 
The 
southerly forty-seven acres have a secondary alternate land use of 
"General Industry," not to exceed 204,732 square feet. 
Eastbluff Remnant 
The Land Use Element of the Newport Beach General Plan designates 
this area for "Low-Density residential" at 4 DU's per buildable 
acre to be transferred to Newport Center. 
This leaves this site 
to function as open space, as shown on the LCP Land Use Plan. 
Mouth of Big Canyon 
The mouth of Big Canyon is shown on the LCP Land Use Plan as "Rec-
reational and Environmental Open Space." 
This area is to be pre-
served in its natural state to enhance the Upper Newport Bay 
Ecological Reserve. 
Newporter North 
This site, located on Jamboree Road northerly of the Newporter Inn, 
is designated for "Low-Density Residential" uses at 4 DU's per 
buildable acre, with 25% of the allowable units transferable to 
either Newport Center or North Ford at the option of the property 
owner. 
The structures shall be clustered to accommodate archaeo-
logical sites and marsh sites. 
A public bikeway/walkway is proposed 


- 52-
for this site. 
Newporter Inn 
The Newporter Inn site has been designated for "Recreational and 
r~arine Commercial ll uses in order to promote the continuation of the 
hotel use and allow expansion if needed. 
Newport Center 
Approximately one-third of the Newport Center site falls within 
the Coastal Zone. 
Most of the area is occupied by the Irvine 
Coast Country Club, shown as IIRecreational and Environmental Open 
Space ll on the Land Use Plan. 
The Marriott Hotel site is designated 
for IIAdministrative, Professional and Financial Commercial
ll uses 
to reflect the hotel use on the site. 
Permitted office uses on 
Corporate Plaza West and the Chamber of Commerce sites are shown 
by the IIAdministrative, Professional and Financial Commercial
ll 
designation, and residential uses are shown on the Sea Island and 
Coast Highway/Jamboree sites by the IIMedium Density Residential!! 
designation. 
Bayview Landing. 
This site adjacent to the Newport Dunes area is designated IIRecrea-
tional and Environmental Open Space 'l for public recreation uses with 
a view park and bike path on the upper portion, and R-V camping on 
the lower portion or similar recreational uses of no greater 
intensity. 
As an alternate use, 4 DU's per buildable acre will be 
permitted to be located on the lower portion of the site to preserve 
existing public views. 
I' 


I 
De Anza Trailer Park 
The De Anza Trailer Park is shown for "Multi-Family Residential" 
uses. 
It is the intent of the City to preserve the trailer park 
use as a means of providing a variety of housing opportunities 
within the City. 
The existing number of mobile home spaces shall 
be maintained by the property owner wither on-site or at another 
site in the City of Newport Beach. 
Beacon Bay 
The Beacon Bay area is a City-owned parcel which is currently 
leased and used for both residential and marine commercial uses. 
The LCP Land Use Plan designations reflect existing uses. 
It is 
the intent of the land use designations to preserve the existing 
land use pattern, particularly the marine commercial uses in the 
Balboa Yacht Basin Area. 
Coast Highwal/Bayside Drive 
This site is currently developed with three major restaurants (the 
Reuben E. Lee, Reuben's, and Isadore's), as well as a yacht broker 
and parking for the docking facilities. 
This area has been desig-
nated for "Recreational and Marine Commercial." 
In addition the 
area to be vacated upon completion of the Newport Bay bridge, shall 
be utilized for a boat launching facility. 
Bayside and Marine 
This vacant site at the entrance to Balboa Island is shown for 
"Recreational and Ma.rine Commercial." 
It is the intent of this 
designation to provide an appropriate land use to be consistent 
with the existing project approved for the site. 
If the office 
project approved for the site cannot be developed, every effort 
shall be made to acquire the site for public open space. 


- 54-
Balboa Island 
No changes to the existing land use pattern on Balboa Island are 
proposed. 
The Marine Avenue and Agate Avenue commercial districts 
are shown for "Retail and Service Commercial" to encourage contin-
uation of the existing patterns. 
Office and residential uses shall 
be prohibited on the ground level, but may be allowed on the second 
level where the ground level is occupied by a primary use which 
provides goods or services directly to the public. 
The submerged 
areas utilized by the Ferry Landing and fuel docks have been 
designated "Recreational and Marine Commercial." 
Only coastally-
dependent uses will be permitted on these bay sites. 
Bayside Drive 
The commercial areas along Bayside Drive below Irvine Terrace are 
shown for "Recreational and Marine Commercial" uses allowing con-
tinuation of the yacht clubs and the Orange County Harbor Depart-
ment facility. 
If redevelopment should occur, uses shall be limited 
to those allowed by this designation. 
Corona del Mar/Coast Highway Commercial 
The Corona del Mar commercial area along Coast Highway is shown for 
"Retail and Service Commercial" uses. 
This area functions primarily 
as a neighborhood commercial area and no changes are being proposed. 
Upper level offices may be permitted consistent with the Retail and 
Service Commercial designation. 
¬£orona del Mar State Beach 
The Corona del Mar State Beach is a primary visitor destination

The des i g nat ion for 
II R e c rea t ion a 1 
and Environmental Open Space" is consistent with the existing use. 
Inspiration Point 
The existing publicly-held parcels, as well as the two undeveloped 
private parcels at Inspiration Point, have been shown for "Recrea-
tional and Environmental Open Space." 
These bluffs constitute a 
resource ~ State-wide significance and as such it is proposed that 
any privately-held vacant parcels in this area be acquired with 
State Coastal Conservancy Funds in order that they be preserved 
for the enjoyment of the public. 
Newport Beach Marine Life Refuge 
The Newport Beach Marine Li fe Refuge is shown for "Recreational and 
Environmental Open Space." 
The purpose of the Marine Life Refuge 
r 
is to conserve the natural flora and fauna resources of the shore-
I 
line, and to protect the marine life in the tide pool areas. 
I 
Buck Gully/Morning Canyon 
The Buck Gully and Morni ng Canyon areas have been shown for"Recrea-
tional and Envi ronmental Open Space" to be preserved in a natural 
state. 
It is proposed that the setbacks of residential lots abut-
ting these areas be adjusted to prevent alteration of the natural 
canyons. 
Upper Newport Bay Ecological Reserve 
This area is designated for "Recreational and Environmental Open 
Space," with detailed plans to be developed by the State Depart-
ment of Fish and Game. 


LAND USE MAPS 
The Land Use Maps which follow illustrate the proposed LPC Land 
Use designations for the Coastal Zone portion of the City. 
The 
City's existing zoning maps are used as a base, with the proposed 
LCP designation as an overlay. 
The existing zoning is illustrated 
with letters (e.g., R-l, R-2, C-l, etc.) and the proposed LCP 
designation is shown with overlay patterns indexed at the bottom 
of each map. 
Existing land use is not shown. 
In many cases the proposed LCP Land Use will not be consistent with 
respect to the LCP zoning regulations when adopted, and would 
probably require Use Permit review for any proposed expansion where 
inconsistencies exist. 
The City's existing zoning ordinance has 
similar provisions for review of nonconforming uses when expanded.

sections.question_synthetic: Can a Planning Commission member who is also a board chairman of a bank participate in matters involving a business that has loans from that bank, and to what extent must he disqualify himself under Government Code sections 87100 and 87103?
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is an incoming request letter (from city attorney to FPPC), not an FPPC response. No conclusion section present. Document includes some unrelated content about coastal-dependent uses and public property leaseholds that appears to be from a different document (poor OCR or scanning artifact). Five specific legal questions enumerated but marked as question I-V in the letter itself.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: I. We have advised the Planning Commission and Mr. Balalis that his vote and participation in matters involving Magic Island, that occurred prior to the time he became aware of the relationship between Magic Island and Newport Harbor National Bank, does not invalidate the proceedings because, at the time of the participation and vote by Mr. Balalis, he did not know, or have reason to know, that he had a financial interest in the matter, and there has been no challenge to the decision of the Planning Commission; II. Is Mr. Balalais precluded from further participation in matters involving Magic Island by virtue of the provisions of Section 87100 and 87103 A, C and/or D; III. Given the size of this lending institution, are there any guidelines that would assist Mr. Ba1ala1is in determining what size of loan commitment, or other business dealing, that will require disqualification; IV. Will Mr. Balalalis be precluded from participating in matters involving persons who have money on deposit with the bank, given the difficult in learning the identity of these persons from day-to-day and the fact that interest rates on deposits are essentially fixed by the Federal Government; V. To what extent is Mr. Balala1is obligated to seek information as to the identify of borrowers, depositors and others who have business dealing with this lending institution?

Box 807 Sacramento, Ca. 95804 Re: Paul Balalis - FPPC Advice Request Dear Ms. Milman: This office is requesting advice from the FPPC concerning Paul Balalis, a member of the Planning Commission for the City of Newport Beach. Since January 19, 1982, Mr. Balalis has served as Chairman of the Board of Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank, and is the owner of approximately 6% of the outstanding shares of stock issued by that institution. Prior to taking this position, and from February 7, 1980, Mr. Balalis was a member of the Board of Directors of Newport Harbor National Bank. This letter is prompted by Mr. Balalis' recent disclosure that he voted on, and participated in the discussions concerning, a Use Permit Application submitted by a business with which the bank had placed approximately $240,000.00 in loans. A more thorough discussion of the relevant facts follows: Magic Island is the name given to a private club located in the City of Newport Beach that attracts members by offering top- quality magic acts, food and a certain ambiance. The business commenced operation on or about September 4, 1981, appoximately one year after the Planning Commission, with Mr. Balalis' vote and participation, approved their Use Permit Application. A Use Permit is a prerequisite to the operation of any restaurant or recreational establishment within the City of Newport Beach. I'-. 1)" : ' { \ Ms. Barbara Milman March 9, 1982 Page Two Re: Paul Balalis - FPPC Advice Request On February 4, 1982, the Planning Commission considered an Application by Magic Island to amend the provisions of the pre- viously granted Use Permit, to allow for daytime use of the facility as well as expansion of the nighttime operation. The Planning Commission approved, on a temporary basis, the daytime use of the facility, and continued the other discretionaryt approvals requested by the business. On or about May 12, 1981, Newport Harbor National Bank placed a loan in the sum of $200,000.00 with Magic Island, the loan guaranteed by Michael Callie and Sally Wrainswaite. It is the understanding of this office that Mr. Callie and Ms. Wrainswaite are principals in the operation and that both have an ownership interest. This loan was renewed November 13, 1981. On or about Auust 26, 1981, a loan in the sum of $100,000.00 was placed with Michael Callie and secured by real property owned by Mr. Callie. This loan was paid September 26, 1981. Each of these loans was made at or above the prime interest rate in effect at the time and, subject to a determination of credit worthiness, were available to other businesses or individuals on the same or similar terms. embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from the City of Newport Beach Assistant City Attorney seeking FPPC advice regarding Paul Balalis, a Planning Commission member and Chairman of Newport Harbor National Bank, regarding his potential conflicts of interest in voting on matters involving Magic Island, a business with approximately $240,000 in loans from the bank.
